title: Anger When You Discover the Pay Gap
dek: You thought you were working just as hard, but the numbers on the screen tell a different story. This is not a misunderstanding; it is a somatic shock that settles deep in your psoas and jaw, demanding to be felt before it can be fought.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Cold Screen and the Hot Jaw


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.234Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You are sitting at your desk, the blue light of the monitor reflecting off your glasses. It is 3:14 PM on a Tuesday, the kind of mid-afternoon slump where the air in the office feels recycled and thin. You open the spreadsheet, the one that was accidentally left unprotected on the shared drive, or perhaps the email thread that was forwarded to you by mistake. You scroll down, your eyes scanning the rows of names and numbers. And then you see it. His name. Your name. His number. Your number. 

The discrepancy is not a few dollars. It is a chasm. It is a canyon carved out of your time, your energy, your life force. 

Notice what happens in your body in this exact second. Before the rationalization begins, before the HR policies are recalled, before the self-doubt creeps in. Notice the sudden, sharp intake of breath. Notice how your masseter muscles - the thick, powerful muscles of your jaw - clench so tightly that your teeth ache. Notice the heat rising in your chest, a sudden flush that spreads up your neck and into your cheeks. This is not a metaphor. This is biology. This is your nervous system registering a threat, an injustice, a violation of the unspoken contract of your labor.

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, anger is the emotion of the Liver. The Liver is responsible for the smooth flow of Qi, of energy, throughout the body. When that flow is obstructed - by injustice, by frustration, by the sudden realization that your work is valued less simply because of the body you inhabit - the Liver Qi stagnates. It builds up, creating heat, creating pressure. You might feel this as a tightness in your ribs, a bitter taste in your mouth, or a sudden, inexplicable headache at the temples. This is your body telling you the truth. 

We are taught, especially as women, to swallow this heat. To drink a glass of cold water, to take a deep breath, to write a polite email asking for a "compensation review." We are taught to intellectualize our fury, to turn it into a bulleted list of our accomplishments, to present it calmly and rationally. But the body knows that this is a lie. The body knows that you have been robbed. 

Bessel van der Kolk, in his foundational work on trauma, reminds us that the body keeps the score. While he often speaks of profound, life-altering traumas, the daily, grinding indignities of systemic inequality also leave their mark on our physiology. The body keeps the score of every unpaid hour, every dismissed idea, every dollar that was rightfully yours but was given to someone else. This score is not kept in a ledger; it is kept in the tension of your trapezius muscles, in the shallow, restricted movement of your diaphragm, in the chronic, low-grade exhaustion that you can never quite sleep off. 

When you discover the pay gap, you are not just discovering a financial discrepancy. You are discovering a somatic reality. You are discovering that the world views your labor, your time, your very existence, as inherently less valuable. And your body, in its infinite wisdom, responds with the only appropriate emotion: rage.

## The Anatomy of Devaluation

Let us look closer at what happens when this rage is suppressed. You close the spreadsheet. You take a deep breath. You tell yourself that you will handle this professionally. You push the heat down. 

Where does it go? It does not simply vanish. Energy cannot be destroyed; it can only change form. The Liver Qi, denied its natural upward and outward expression, turns inward. It settles in the psoas, the deep muscle of the core that connects your spine to your legs. The psoas is the muscle of the fight-or-flight response; it is the muscle that contracts to curl you into a fetal position to protect your vital organs. When you suppress your anger, your psoas tightens, preparing for a fight that you are not allowing yourself to have. 

You might notice this as a dull ache in your lower back, a stiffness in your hips when you stand up from your desk. You might notice that your breathing becomes shallow, restricted to the upper chest, because a tight psoas pulls on the diaphragm, preventing it from descending fully. You are literally suffocating your own life force to maintain the illusion of professional composure.

This is where the herbs come in. Not as a cure, not as a way to numb the pain, but as allies in the process of moving the stagnant energy. A strong infusion of Yarrow (Achillea millefolium), a plant named for the warrior Achilles, can help to move the blood, to break up the stagnation. Yarrow is a diaphoretic; it opens the pores and allows the heat to escape. It is a bitter herb, and the bitter taste stimulates the Liver, encouraging it to release its pent-up energy. You drink the Yarrow tea, and you allow yourself to sweat. You allow the heat to move. 

But herbs alone are not enough. You must also engage with the somatic reality of your anger. You must allow the body to complete the stress cycle that was initiated when you saw those numbers on the screen. 

Resmaa Menakem, in his profound work on somatic abolitionism, teaches us that trauma and oppression are not just historical events or cognitive concepts; they are lived experiences that are passed down and stored in the body, specifically in the nervous system. He speaks of the "white body" and the "Black body," but his principles apply to all bodies that have been marginalized, devalued, or oppressed. The pay gap is a form of systemic oppression, and it lands in the body as a somatic shock. Menakem emphasizes the need to settle the nervous system, not by ignoring the injustice, but by building the capacity to hold the charge of the anger without being consumed by it. 

We must learn to track the sensations of our anger, to notice the constriction, the heat, the vibration, and to stay with it, rather than dissociating or acting out impulsively. This is the work of somatic awareness. It is the work of reclaiming your body from a system that seeks to extract your labor while denying your worth.

## The Illusion of the Meritocracy

The anger you feel is compounded by the myth you have been sold. The myth of the meritocracy. The myth that if you just work hard enough, if you just lean in, if you just get the right degree, the right certification, the right mentor, you will be rewarded equally. 

When you see the numbers, the myth shatters. And the shattering is not just cognitive; it is visceral. It is the feeling of the ground dropping out from under you. It is the sudden, nauseating realization that the rules you have been playing by are rigged. 

This realization often brings with it a wave of shame. You might think, "I should have negotiated better." "I should have known." "I am stupid for believing them." Notice this shame. Notice how it feels different from the anger. The anger is hot, expansive, upward-moving. The shame is cold, contracting, downward-moving. It makes you want to hide, to shrink, to disappear. 

Do not let the shame win. The shame is a tool of the system, designed to keep you quiet, to keep you isolated, to keep you from sharing those numbers with other women. 

Pema Chödrön, the Tibetan Buddhist nun and teacher, speaks often of "shenpa," a Tibetan word that is usually translated as "attachment," but which she describes more accurately as "getting hooked." It is the urge, the tug, the sudden tightening in the belly when we are triggered. When you discover the pay gap, you are hooked. You are hooked by the injustice, by the betrayal, by the shame. 

Chödrön teaches that the practice is not to avoid getting hooked - that is impossible in a world as broken as ours - but to notice when we are hooked, and to pause. To not immediately bite the hook and spin off into a story of our own inadequacy or a blind rage that burns down our own lives. The practice is to stay with the raw, uncomfortable energy of the shenpa, to feel the tightening in the belly, the heat in the face, and to breathe into it. 

This is not a passive acceptance of the pay gap. It is the exact opposite. It is the gathering of your power. When you refuse to be consumed by the shame, when you refuse to act out of blind, ungrounded rage, you are conserving your energy for the fight ahead. You are building the somatic capacity to demand what is yours.

## The Somatic Practice of Demanding Your Worth

How do we move from the shock of discovery to the grounded action of demanding our worth? We must start with the body. We must start by un-clenching the jaw, by releasing the psoas, by allowing the breath to drop deep into the belly. 

Here is a short embodied practice to help you move the energy of this specific anger. 

Find a place where you can be alone and uninterrupted. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, your knees slightly bent. Feel the solid ground beneath your feet. Imagine that you have roots extending from the soles of your feet deep into the earth. 

Bring your attention to your jaw. Notice the tension there. Slowly, deliberately, open your mouth as wide as you can, as if you are about to scream, but do not make a sound. Stretch the masseter muscles. Hold this for a few seconds, then release. Repeat this three times. 

Next, bring your attention to your hands. Clench them into tight fists. Feel the tension in your forearms, your biceps, your shoulders. This is the energy of the fight. Hold the fists tight, and take a deep breath in. As you exhale, forcefully throw your hands down toward the ground, opening your fingers wide, and make a loud "HA!" sound. 

Do this again. Clench the fists, inhale, and throw the energy down with a "HA!" 

Do this until you feel a shift in your energy, until the heat in your chest begins to dissipate, until you feel more grounded, more present in your body. 

Now, place one hand on your heart and one hand on your belly. Feel the rise and fall of your breath. Say to yourself, out loud: "My labor is valuable. My time is valuable. I am worthy of equal compensation." 

Notice how these words feel in your body. Do they feel true? Do they feel forced? If there is resistance, notice where it lives. Is it a tightness in the throat? A fluttering in the stomach? Breathe into that resistance. Acknowledge it. It is the internalized voice of the system, and it will take time to dismantle.

## The Herbal Allies for the Long Fight

The fight for equal pay is not a sprint; it is a marathon. It requires sustained energy, clear boundaries, and a resilient nervous system. We cannot rely on caffeine and adrenaline to sustain us; they will only deplete our Kidney Jing, our deep foundational energy, leaving us burned out and exhausted. 

We need the deep, nourishing support of the adaptogens. Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera), a root used in Ayurvedic medicine for thousands of years, is a powerful ally here. It helps to regulate the HPA axis, the communication system between the hypothalamus, the pituitary gland, and the adrenal glands, which controls our stress response. Ashwagandha helps to lower cortisol levels, to soothe the frayed edges of the nervous system, and to build deep, enduring strength. 

You might also consider Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca). The Latin name translates to "lion-hearted," and this is exactly what this herb provides. It is a bitter mint that acts as a nervine and a mild cardiovascular tonic. It is specifically indicated for the kind of anxiety that manifests as palpitations, a racing heart, a feeling of panic in the chest. Motherwort helps to steady the heart, to calm the spirit, and to give you the courage of a lioness when you walk into that HR office to demand your worth. 

I recommend taking these herbs not as a quick fix, but as a daily practice. A dropperful of Motherwort tincture before a difficult meeting. A cup of Ashwagandha milk in the evening to help you sleep. Let the plants remind you of your own inherent strength, your own connection to the earth, your own undeniable worth. 

You might also want to explore the use of [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) to support your nervous system during this time. It is a potent ally for the heart and the spirit. And for the deep, restorative sleep that is so often disrupted by the stress of workplace injustice, a high-quality [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be invaluable. 

## The Conversation with the System

When you finally have the conversation - with your boss, with HR, with the powers that be - you must bring your body with you. Do not leave your body at the door. Do not try to be a floating head, a purely rational, emotionless machine. 

They will expect you to be polite. They will expect you to be grateful for what you have. They will use words like "budget constraints," "market rates," "experience levels." They will try to gaslight you into believing that the discrepancy is your fault, or that it doesn't really exist, or that it is simply the way things are. 

Gabor Maté, the physician and author who has written extensively on the connection between stress, trauma, and illness, points out that in our society, we are often forced to choose between attachment and authenticity. Attachment is our need for connection, for belonging, for approval. Authenticity is our connection to our true selves, our gut feelings, our somatic reality. When we are children, if our authenticity threatens our attachment to our caregivers, we will suppress our authenticity to survive. 

In the workplace, this dynamic is recreated. We suppress our authentic anger, our authentic demand for fairness, in order to maintain our attachment to the job, to the paycheck, to the approval of our superiors. Maté argues that this chronic suppression of authenticity is a major driver of autoimmune disease, chronic pain, and depression. 

When you walk into that meeting, you are choosing authenticity over a false attachment. You are choosing to honor the truth of your body over the lies of the system. 

Feel your feet on the floor. Feel the support of the chair beneath you. Keep your breathing low and slow. If you feel the heat rising, if you feel the tears of frustration pricking at your eyes, do not panic. Let the energy move. You can say, "I am feeling very strongly about this, because it is a matter of fundamental fairness." You do not have to yell, but you do not have to whisper, either. Let your voice resonate from your chest, not just your throat. 

## The Aftermath and the Integration

Whether the conversation results in an immediate raise, a promise of a future review, or a flat denial, the somatic work continues. The adrenaline will crash. The vulnerability hangover will set in. You will likely feel exhausted, shaky, perhaps even more angry than before. 

This is the time for deep care. This is the time to draw a hot bath, to add Epsom salts and a few drops of lavender essential oil. This is the time to wrap yourself in a heavy blanket, to drink a cup of strong, nourishing tea, like a long-steeped infusion of Nettle (Urtica dioica) and Oatstraw (Avena sativa). These herbs are rich in minerals, in calcium, magnesium, and iron, the very building blocks of a resilient nervous system. 

You have done a brave thing. You have spoken the truth of your body to a system that relies on your silence. You have disrupted the smooth functioning of the machine. 

The pay gap is not just a financial issue; it is a spiritual and somatic wound. It is a daily reminder that the world does not value you equally. But your anger is the medicine. Your anger is the fire that will burn down the illusion of the meritocracy and forge a new reality, one where your labor, your time, and your body are honored and compensated fairly. 

Keep the fire burning. Do not let them extinguish it with their corporate speak and their empty promises. Tend to the fire with the herbs, with the somatic practices, with the fierce, unyielding love for your own body and your own worth. The [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a daily ritual of remineralization, a way of putting back what the system has extracted. 

## The Deeper Roots of the Discrepancy

Let us sink deeper into the fascia of this issue. The pay gap is not a modern invention; it is an ancient inheritance. It is the financial manifestation of a millennia-old belief that the bodies of women, and the bodies of the marginalized, are resources to be extracted rather than sovereign entities to be respected. When you look at that spreadsheet, you are not just seeing your own underpayment; you are seeing the ghost of every woman who was told her work was "just helping out," every woman whose labor in the home was entirely uncompensated, every woman who was barred from owning property or holding a bank account. 

This history lives in your cells. It is part of the epigenetic inheritance that shapes how your nervous system responds to threat. When you feel that sudden, sickening drop in your stomach upon seeing the numbers, you are feeling the collective somatic memory of devaluation. 

Notice how this memory shapes your posture. Do your shoulders round forward, protecting your heart? Does your neck jut out, straining to see the danger ahead? This is the posture of submission, the posture of the prey animal trying to make itself small. The system relies on you adopting this posture. It relies on you shrinking, apologizing, accommodating. 

To demand equal pay is to demand a change in posture. It is to roll the shoulders back, to open the chest, to align the spine so that the energy can flow freely from the earth to the crown of the head. This is not just a physical adjustment; it is a profound act of rebellion. It is a declaration that you will no longer make yourself small to make the system comfortable. 

Consider the role of the vagus nerve in this process. The vagus nerve is the superhighway of the parasympathetic nervous system, the system responsible for rest, digest, and social engagement. When we are chronically stressed, chronically devalued, the vagus nerve loses its tone. We become stuck in fight-or-flight, or worse, in freeze. We dissociate from our bodies because the reality of the injustice is too painful to bear. 

Somatic practices that tone the vagus nerve - such as deep, slow diaphragmatic breathing, humming, chanting, or even splashing cold water on your face - are essential tools in the fight for equal pay. They help to bring you back into your body, back into the present moment, so that you can respond to the injustice with clarity and power, rather than reacting from a place of triggered trauma. 

You must become a student of your own nervous system. You must learn to recognize the subtle signs of dysregulation - the shallow breath, the tight chest, the racing thoughts - and you must have the tools to bring yourself back to center. This is the foundation of all effective activism, whether it is in the streets or in the HR office. 

## The Alchemy of Resentment

If anger is the fire that fuels the demand for justice, resentment is the slow, smoldering ash that chokes the life out of you. Resentment is anger that has been swallowed, digested, and turned toxic. It is the feeling of being a victim, of being powerless to change your circumstances. 

When you discover the pay gap, it is incredibly easy to slip into resentment. You might find yourself snapping at your partner, complaining endlessly to your friends, or engaging in passive-aggressive behavior at work. You might start doing the bare minimum, not out of a conscious choice to set boundaries, but out of a deep, unarticulated bitterness. 

Resentment is a poison to the Liver. It creates a thick, sticky stagnation that is much harder to move than the hot, explosive energy of acute anger. It manifests physically as chronic fatigue, digestive issues, a feeling of heaviness in the limbs, and a dull, persistent ache in the joints. 

To transform resentment back into clean, actionable anger, you must first acknowledge it. You must admit that you feel powerless, that you feel victimized. And then, you must take one small, concrete action to reclaim your agency. 

This action does not have to be quitting your job or filing a lawsuit. It can be as simple as updating your resume. It can be researching the market rate for your position. It can be having an honest conversation with a trusted colleague about salary transparency. 

Every time you take an action that affirms your worth, you move the stagnant energy of resentment. You fan the smoldering ash back into a bright, clean flame. You remind your body that you are not a victim, that you are a powerful, sovereign being capable of demanding what is yours. 

The herbs can support this alchemy. A tincture of Dandelion root (Taraxacum officinale) is a profound Liver alterative. It helps the Liver to process and eliminate toxins, both physical and emotional. It is a bitter, grounding root that reminds us of our connection to the earth, of our inherent resilience. Dandelion grows in the cracks of the pavement; it thrives in the most hostile environments. It teaches us how to survive, and even flourish, in a system that was not designed for our benefit. 

Take a dropperful of Dandelion root tincture before meals. As you taste the bitterness, visualize the stagnant energy of resentment being broken down, processed, and eliminated from your body. Visualize the clean, bright energy of righteous anger taking its place. 

## The Collective Body

Finally, we must remember that the pay gap is not an individual failing; it is a systemic reality. And systemic realities cannot be dismantled by individuals acting alone. They must be dismantled by the collective body. 

When you discover the pay gap, your first instinct might be to hide it, to keep it a secret. The system relies on this secrecy. It relies on the shame and the isolation that keep us from sharing our numbers, from comparing our experiences, from realizing the full extent of the injustice. 

To break the silence is to break the spell. When you share your numbers with other women, with other marginalized people in your workplace, you are not just sharing data; you are sharing power. You are weaving a web of solidarity that the system cannot easily tear apart. 

This is the somatic experience of belonging. When you realize that you are not alone, that your anger is shared, that your devaluation is part of a larger pattern, the burden shifts. It no longer rests solely on your shoulders. It is distributed across the collective body. 

You feel this shift in your physiology. The chronic tension in your neck and shoulders begins to ease. The breath deepens. The heart rate slows. You are no longer a solitary prey animal scanning the horizon for danger; you are part of a herd, a pack, a collective force that is capable of defending itself and demanding its due. 

This is the ultimate goal of the somatic work. Not just to heal the individual body, but to build the capacity of the collective body to resist, to disrupt, and to create a new reality. A reality where the labor of all bodies is valued equally, where the earth is respected, where the deep, ancient wisdom of the body is honored as the ultimate truth. 

Until that day comes, we will keep the fire burning. We will drink the bitter herbs. We will un-clench our jaws. We will breathe deep into our bellies. And we will demand, with every fiber of our being, the worth that is inherently ours.

## FAQ

**Q: I found out my male coworker makes 30% more than me for the exact same job. I am so angry I can't even look at him. Is it wrong to be mad at him?**

**A:** Your anger is a completely natural, biological response to a profound injustice. It is not "wrong" to feel it. However, it is important to track where the anger is directed. Your coworker is a beneficiary of the system, but he is not the architect of the system. The system itself - the company, the culture of secrecy around pay, the patriarchal devaluation of women's labor - is the true source of the harm. When you feel the rage rising when you look at him, notice the sensation in your body. Is it a tightening in the chest? A clenching of the jaw? Breathe into that sensation. Acknowledge the unfairness. Then, consciously redirect that fiery energy toward the structures that created the disparity. Use the energy to document your achievements, to research market rates, and to prepare for a conversation with the people who actually control the budget. Do not waste your precious Liver Qi on resenting a pawn; save it for the kings and queens of the board.

**Q: I tried to have a conversation with HR about the pay gap, and I started crying. I feel so embarrassed and weak. How can I stop crying when I'm angry?**

**A:** You are not weak. You are experiencing a physiological phenomenon that is incredibly common, especially for women who have been socialized to suppress their anger. When the intense, upward-moving energy of Liver Qi (anger) meets the heavy, downward-moving energy of suppression or fear, the pressure often releases through the tear ducts. It is a pressure valve. The tears are not a sign of sadness; they are a sign of overwhelming somatic charge. The embarrassment you feel is the shame of the system, which demands clinical detachment and punishes emotional expression. The next time you feel the tears coming, do not fight them. Fighting them only increases the tension in your throat and chest. Instead, ground yourself. Feel your feet on the floor. Wiggle your toes. Press your hands firmly onto your thighs. You can even say out loud, "I am crying because I am incredibly frustrated and angry about this injustice, not because I am sad." Naming the emotion reclaims your power and forces the other person to deal with the reality of your anger, rather than dismissing you as "emotional."

**Q: I've been fighting for equal pay for years, and I'm just exhausted. My body hurts, I can't sleep, and I feel like giving up. What herbs can help with this burnout?**

**A:** What you are describing is a profound depletion of your vital energy, what TCM would call Kidney Yin and Qi deficiency, coupled with the chronic stagnation of Liver Qi. You have been running on adrenaline and righteous indignation for too long, and the well is running dry. Before you can continue the fight, you must rebuild the foundation. This is not the time for stimulating herbs or intense somatic release. This is the time for deep, restorative nourishment. I recommend a daily infusion of Oatstraw (Avena sativa) and Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora). Oatstraw is a supreme trophorestorative for the nervous system, rich in minerals that rebuild the myelin sheaths around your nerves. Skullcap is a gentle, grounding nervine that helps to quiet the looping, anxious thoughts that keep you awake at night. Drink a quart of this infusion daily. Prioritize rest, even if it feels unproductive. Your body is a battlefield, and it needs time to heal before it can march again.

**Q: Is it possible to heal the somatic wounds of the pay gap even if I never get the raise I deserve?**

**A:** Yes. The healing does not come from the corporation validating your worth; the healing comes from you validating your own worth. The trauma of the pay gap is the trauma of being devalued and internalizing that devaluation. The somatic healing occurs when you refuse to carry that devaluation in your tissues anymore. It occurs when you release the tension in your psoas, when you un-clench your jaw, when you allow your breath to take up space in your torso. It occurs when you set fierce boundaries around your time and energy, refusing to do the unpaid emotional labor or the extra tasks that are expected of you. You may have to leave the job to find fair compensation, or you may choose to stay for other reasons, but you can do so with a nervous system that is grounded in its own inherent dignity, rather than one that is constantly begging for scraps from a table that was never set for you.
